cards.text: A {{c1::signed}} integer can hold both {{c2::positive}} and {{c3::negative}} whole numbers, including {{c4::zero}}.

cards.front: What are the four primary signed integer types in C++?

cards.text: By default, the keyword {{c1::int}} is equivalent to {{c2::signed int}}. The signed keyword is usually {{c3::omitted}}.

cards.front: What is the minimum guaranteed range for a signed int in C++?
cards.back:
-32,768 to 32,767 (16-bit, 2 bytes minimum)
In practice, usually -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 (32-bit, 4 bytes)

cards.text: Best practice: Prefer {{c1::int}} when storing integer values (unless space is at a premium).

cards.question: What happens with this code (assuming 4-byte int)?
cards.explanation: Adding 1 to the maximum int value causes signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior in C++. The result is unpredictable.

cards.text: {{c1::Integer overflow}} occurs when a value is outside the range the type can store, resulting in {{c2::undefined behavior}}.

cards.front: What is the result of dividing two integers in C++?
cards.back:
Integer division: the result is an integer with the fractional part dropped.
Example: 7 / 3 = 2 (not 2.33...)

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: Integer division truncates the decimal part. 7 / 3 = 2.33... becomes 2.

cards.concept: When to use short, int, long, and long long
cards.explanation:
Integer type selection:
  • int: Default choice for most integers (fastest on most systems)
  • short: When saving memory is critical (e.g., large arrays)
  • long/long long: When you need guaranteed larger ranges
examples: long long bigNum{ 9999999999 }; // ok: need large value

cards.text: C++ does not guarantee an 8-bit signed integer type, but provides {{c1::std::int8_t}} if available on the system.

cards.front: What are two ways to avoid integer overflow?
cards.back:
• Use a larger integer type (e.g., long long instead of int)
  • Validate inputs and calculations before performing them
